title: Ulf Kristersson

infobox.value: (Swedish: [[ˈɵ̂lf ˈkrɪstɛˌʂɔn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Swedish "Help:IPA/Swedish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q9027_(swe)-Moonhouse-Ulf_Kristersson.wav "File:LL-Q9027 (swe)-Moonhouse-Ulf Kristersson.wav"); born 29 December 1963) is a Swedish politician who has served as [Prime Minister of Sweden](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Prime Minister of Sweden") since 2022 and as [Leader of the Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party") since 2017. He has been a [Member of the Riksdag](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Sweden) "Member of Parliament (Sweden)") for [Södermanland County](/wiki/S%C3%B6dermanland_County "Södermanland County") since 2014, previously holding a seat for [Stockholm County](/wiki/Stockholm_County "Stockholm County") from 1991 to 2000.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He also served as [Minister for Social Security](/wiki/Minister_for_Social_Security_(Sweden) "Minister for Social Security (Sweden)") from 2010 to 2014 and as chairman of the [Moderate Youth League](/wiki/Moderate_Youth_League "Moderate Youth League") from 1988 to 1992.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Ulf Hjalmar Kristersson** (Swedish: [[ˈɵ̂lf ˈkrɪstɛˌʂɔn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Swedish "Help:IPA/Swedish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q9027_(swe)-Moonhouse-Ulf_Kristersson.wav "File:LL-Q9027 (swe)-Moonhouse-Ulf Kristersson.wav"); born 29 December 1963) is a Swedish politician who has served as [Prime Minister of Sweden](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Prime Minister of Sweden") since 2022 and as [Leader of the Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party") since 2017. He has been a [Member of the Riksdag](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Sweden) "Member of Parliament (Sweden)") for [Södermanland County](/wiki/S%C3%B6dermanland_County "Södermanland County") since 2014, previously holding a seat for [Stockholm County](/wiki/Stockholm_County "Stockholm County") from 1991 to 2000.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He also served as [Minister for Social Security](/wiki/Minister_for_Social_Security_(Sweden) "Minister for Social Security (Sweden)") from 2010 to 2014 and as chairman of the [Moderate Youth League](/wiki/Moderate_Youth_League "Moderate Youth League") from 1988 to 1992.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

On 11 December 2014, he was appointed shadow [Finance Minister](/wiki/Minister_for_Finance_(Sweden) "Minister for Finance (Sweden)") of the Moderate Party and economic policy spokesperson. On 1 October 2017, Kristersson was elected party leader after [Anna Kinberg Batra](/wiki/Anna_Kinberg_Batra "Anna Kinberg Batra") stepped down.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Under his leadership, the Moderate Party opened up to the [Sweden Democrats](/wiki/Sweden_Democrats "Sweden Democrats") (SD) and, by late 2021, had formed an informal right-wing coalition with them together with two other centre-right parties and former members of the dissolved [Alliance](/wiki/Alliance_(Sweden) "Alliance (Sweden)"). In the [2022 Swedish general election](/wiki/2022_Swedish_general_election "2022 Swedish general election"), that bloc, later organised under the [Tidö Agreement](/wiki/Tid%C3%B6_Agreement "Tidö Agreement"), obtained a majority in the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag"), leading to Kristersson's election as prime minister on 17 October.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

As prime minister, Kristersson oversaw [Sweden's accession to NATO](/wiki/Sweden-NATO_relations "Sweden-NATO relations"), as well as announcing the largest rearmament of the [Swedish Armed Forces](/wiki/Swedish_Armed_Forces "Swedish Armed Forces") since the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). His administration has also taken action on the [migration to Sweden](/wiki/Immigration_to_Sweden "Immigration to Sweden").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life")]

Ulf Kristersson was born in Lund, Skåne County, as the eldest of three children to Lars Kristersson (1937–2015) who worked with economics and teacher Karin Kristersson (née Axelsson; 1939–2020).[[6]](#cite_note-auto-6) The family moved to [Torshälla](/wiki/Torsh%C3%A4lla "Torshälla") outside [Eskilstuna](/wiki/Eskilstuna "Eskilstuna") five years later.[[7]](#cite_note-ekuriren.se-7) In his youth, Kristersson was a troupe gymnast.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
Kristersson finished secondary school at S:t Eskils gymnasium in [Eskilstuna](/wiki/Eskilstuna "Eskilstuna"). After graduating, Kristersson did military service as a platoon commander in the [Uppland Regiment](/wiki/Uppland_Regiment_(signals) "Uppland Regiment (signals)") from 1983 to 1984[[9]](#cite_note-9) and completed a degree in economics at [Uppsala University](/wiki/Uppsala_University "Uppsala University").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Early political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early political career")]

In connection with the [1985 Swedish general election](/wiki/1985_Swedish_general_election "1985 Swedish general election"), he was employed as a campaigner at the Moderate Youth League (MUF) in [Sörmland](/wiki/S%C3%B6rmland "Sörmland").[[11]](#cite_note-11) On 26 November 1988, he rose to become the new Chairman of MUF, succeeding [Beatrice Ask](/wiki/Beatrice_Ask "Beatrice Ask").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1991, the centre-right [Bildt Cabinet](/wiki/Bildt_Cabinet "Bildt Cabinet") took power, and Kristersson became an MP.[[13]](#cite_note-13) He served on the Social Security Committee. He soon becomes a vocal critic of the government's crisis agreement with [Social Democrats](/wiki/Swedish_Social_Democratic_Party "Swedish Social Democratic Party"). At the time, Kristersson developed a friendship with the former party leader, [Gösta Bohman](/wiki/G%C3%B6sta_Bohman "Gösta Bohman"), who, in some respects, also supported his criticism of the [Bildt Cabinet](/wiki/Bildt_Cabinet "Bildt Cabinet").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1992, Kristersson was challenged as chairman of MUF by [Fredrik Reinfeldt](/wiki/Fredrik_Reinfeldt "Fredrik Reinfeldt").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The congress was preceded by considerable ideological divisions between liberals and [conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism"). All this erupted at the congress in [Lycksele](/wiki/Lycksele "Lycksele"), which came to be known as the [Battle of Lycksele](/wiki/Battle_of_Lycksele "Battle of Lycksele").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Kristersson, the liberal alternative, lost narrowly. It is said that his loss caused his withdrawal from front-line politics and he was subsequently known as part of the "Lost Generation" of the [Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party").[[17]](#cite_note-aftonbladet.se-17) At the time, he was criticised for his amateurism and preference for communication over political thought.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18)

From 1995 to 1998, Kristersson was chief of marketing at [Timbro](/wiki/Timbro "Timbro"), a [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") think-tank, while also working in parliament. In 1994 he also released the book *Non-working Generation* at Timbros publishing company. In the book Kristersson argues against the welfare institutions in Sweden and compares these to apartheid because he considered these institutions to force people into passivity.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Career outside politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Career outside politics")]

Kristersson left his parliamentary seat in April 2000, feeling that the new party leader [Bo Lundgren](/wiki/Bo_Lundgren "Bo Lundgren") had declined his services.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Kristersson worked for two years in the private sector, mainly as communications director and VP for the internet consultancy Adcore,[[21]](#cite_note-21) a dotcom crash casualty.

Kristersson was chairman of the Swedish Adoption Centre (*Adoptionscenter*). During his time as chairman, information emerged that the centre handled adoptions of children trafficked from China.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Municipal politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Municipal politics")]

He returned to active politics in 2002 as Commissioner (Mayor) for Finance in [Strängnäs](/wiki/Str%C3%A4ngn%C3%A4s "Strängnäs") and served there until 2006.[[7]](#cite_note-ekuriren.se-7) In 2006, he was appointed Vice Mayor (*Socialborgarråd*) in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), responsible for the social welfare and labor division.[[6]](#cite_note-auto-6)

During this time Kristersson got a rental contract for a five-room apartment in central Stockholm from [Ersta Diakoni](/w/index.php?title=Ersta_Diakoni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ersta Diakoni (page does not exist)") [[sv](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ersta_Diakoni "sv:Ersta Diakoni")]. Ersta Diakoni describes its basic purpose as "to be a support for people in vulnerable situations, to take social responsibility and to offer care."[[23]](#cite_note-23) Due to that, Stockholm city was contracting and gave economical aid to Ersta Diakoni, that, among other things provided for housing for those in social need. An investigation was started and Kristersson and another person in the associations leadership were suspected of bribery. According to an internal policy document, the apartments in the building were reserved for those newly employed by the association and students at Marie Cederschiöld högskola. The investigation was closed with the motivation that Kristersson did not have direct influence over the aid that the association could give.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18)

Fredrik Reinfeldt also asked Kristersson to lead the committee responsible for developing a new family policy for the party.[[17]](#cite_note-aftonbladet.se-17) He immediately caused controversy by suggesting that fathers must take a month of paternity leave for the family to receive all benefits.[[7]](#cite_note-ekuriren.se-7) This was clearly conflicted with traditional [Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party") policy, which has centred on individual choice.[[7]](#cite_note-ekuriren.se-7)

### Return to national politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Return to national politics")]

On 5 October 2010, [Fredrik Reinfeldt](/wiki/Fredrik_Reinfeldt "Fredrik Reinfeldt") appointed Kristersson to become Minister of Social Security, a position he held for four years.[[25]](#cite_note-25) After the [2014 Swedish general election](/wiki/2014_Swedish_general_election "2014 Swedish general election"), the Reinfeldt cabinet resigned, but Kristersson was elected as MP again, this time for [Södermanland County](/wiki/S%C3%B6dermanland_County "Södermanland County").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Following Reinfeldt's resignation as party leader, [Anna Kinberg Batra](/wiki/Anna_Kinberg_Batra "Anna Kinberg Batra") appointed him as [Shadow](/wiki/Shadow_cabinet "Shadow cabinet") Finance Minister.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Leader of the Moderate Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Leader of the Moderate Party")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Ulf_Kristersson_in_2018_Swedish_general_election%2C_2018.jpg/250px-Ulf_Kristersson_in_2018_Swedish_general_election%2C_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ulf_Kristersson_in_2018_Swedish_general_election,_2018.jpg)

Kristersson in the [2018 Swedish general election](/wiki/2018_Swedish_general_election "2018 Swedish general election").

[Anna Kinberg Batra](/wiki/Anna_Kinberg_Batra "Anna Kinberg Batra") resigned as leader of the [Moderates](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party") on 25 August 2017, after internal criticism.[[28]](#cite_note-france24.com-28) Kristersson publicly decided to run for leadership on 1 September and was elected on 1 October.[[29]](#cite_note-bbc.com-29)[[30]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-30) The party saw a sharp increase in support in the polls, compared to the record low numbers under his predecessor Batra.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[28]](#cite_note-france24.com-28) He has a harsher stance against immigration than his predecessors.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-30)

### 2018–2019 government formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2018–2019 government formation")]

Main article: [2018–2019 Swedish government formation](/wiki/2018%E2%80%932019_Swedish_government_formation "2018–2019 Swedish government formation")

In September 2018, incumbent Prime Minister [Stefan Löfven](/wiki/Stefan_L%C3%B6fven "Stefan Löfven") was ousted.[[29]](#cite_note-bbc.com-29) Kristersson expressed hope of becoming the next prime minister.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[29]](#cite_note-bbc.com-29) On 2 October, he was designated by Speaker of the Riksdag [Andreas Norlén](/wiki/Andreas_Norl%C3%A9n "Andreas Norlén") to form a new government.[[34]](#cite_note-34) He initially sought to form a government coalition involving the [Alliance](/wiki/Alliance_(Sweden) "Alliance (Sweden)") parties ([Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party"), [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Sweden) "Centre Party (Sweden)"), [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democrats_(Sweden) "Christian Democrats (Sweden)") and [Liberals](/wiki/Liberals_(Sweden) "Liberals (Sweden)")) with support from the [Swedish Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Swedish_Social_Democratic_Party "Swedish Social Democratic Party") (S). On 9 October, he said that S had rejected all further talks on agreements and that he would now seek other ways to form a new government.[[35]](#cite_note-35) On 14 October, he stated that he was not able to form a new government.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

On 5 November 2018, Speaker Norlén proposed Kristersson as prime minister following breakdowns in all other government negotiations.[[37]](#cite_note-37) On 14 November 2018, the Riksdag rejected Kristersson's bid to become prime minister by a vote of 195 to 154. It was the first time ever that a speaker's proposal for Prime Minister lost such a vote and the first time in 40 years that centre-right parties (Centre Party and Liberals) vetoed a centre-right candidate for Prime Minister.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### 2019–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2019–present")]

Kristersson held a meeting in December 2019 with [Jimmie Åkesson](/wiki/Jimmie_%C3%85kesson "Jimmie Åkesson"), leader of the [Sweden Democrats](/wiki/Sweden_Democrats "Sweden Democrats"), and said that he would cooperate with them in parliament. The anti-immigration party had previously been subject to a *[cordon sanitaire](/wiki/Cordon_sanitaire_(politics) "Cordon sanitaire (politics)")* by all other parties, with Kristersson himself ruling out dialogue with them ahead of the 2018 elections. According to Ann-Cathrine Jungar of [Södertörn University](/wiki/S%C3%B6dert%C3%B6rn_University "Södertörn University"), this put Sweden in line with several other European countries in which centre-right and radical-right parties cooperate.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In August 2020, he criticised the government for a perceived failure to deal with rising [crime](/wiki/Crime_in_Sweden "Crime in Sweden"), including [gun violence](/wiki/Gun_violence_in_Sweden "Gun violence in Sweden"), which he called a "second pandemic".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

#### 2021 government crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2021 government crisis")]

Main article: [2021 Swedish government crisis](/wiki/2021_Swedish_government_crisis "2021 Swedish government crisis")

On 29 June 2021, after Prime Minister [Stefan Löfven](/wiki/Stefan_L%C3%B6fven "Stefan Löfven") was ousted, Speaker of the Riksdag [Andreas Norlén](/wiki/Andreas_Norl%C3%A9n "Andreas Norlén") formally tasked Kristersson with forming a government. Kristersson had until 3 July to report his potential government to Norlén.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Kristersson planned to lead a coalition of his own party along with the Christian Democrats, Liberals, and Sweden Democrats. On 1 July, Kristersson informed the Speaker that he did not have enough support to form a government and returned his mandate.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### 2022 government formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2022 government formation")]

Main article: [2022 Swedish general election](/wiki/2022_Swedish_general_election "2022 Swedish general election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Ulf_Kristersson_in_october_2022_%285_av_7%29.jpg/250px-Ulf_Kristersson_in_october_2022_%285_av_7%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ulf_Kristersson_in_october_2022_(5_av_7).jpg)

Kristersson going to meet [the Speaker](/wiki/Andreas_Norl%C3%A9n "Andreas Norlén"), October 2022.

Kristersson led the [Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party") (M) during the 2022 campaign, in which his party lost parliamentary seats, as well as the second-place position (for the first time since the [1976 Swedish general election](/wiki/1976_Swedish_general_election "1976 Swedish general election")); nonetheless, the right-wing bloc gained an absolute majority, resulting in [Magdalena Andersson](/wiki/Magdalena_Andersson "Magdalena Andersson")'s resignation and Kristersson's nomination as prime minister by Speaker [Andreas Norlén](/wiki/Andreas_Norl%C3%A9n "Andreas Norlén").[[44]](#cite_note-44) Kristersson signalled his preference for a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") between M, the [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democrats_(Sweden) "Christian Democrats (Sweden)") (KD) and [Liberals](/wiki/Liberals_(Sweden) "Liberals (Sweden)") (L) with external support from the [Sweden Democrats](/wiki/Sweden_Democrats "Sweden Democrats") (SD).[[45]](#cite_note-45)

On 14 October, Kristersson presented the fully documented [Tidö Agreement](/wiki/Tid%C3%B6_Agreement "Tidö Agreement") (*Tidöavtalet*) between M, KD, L and SD.[[46]](#cite_note-:0-46) This enabled the first three parties to seek a mandate for a new national administration to be chosen by the Riksdag, with SD given strong influence amounting to [confidence and supply](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply").[[46]](#cite_note-:0-46) On 17 October, Kristersson was elected prime minister by 176 Riksdag votes for, and 173 votes against him, with no absentees or abstentions.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) It is the first time SD has exerted direct government influence.[[49]](#cite_note-49) European Union lawmakers criticised the center-right and the Moderate Party in particular, as a member of the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party"), for allying with the far right,[[50]](#cite_note-50) as did opposition leaders.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The joint programme places particular emphasis on fighting crime, reducing [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Sweden "Immigration to Sweden") and reviving [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_energy_policy "Nuclear energy policy"). The quota of [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") accepted each year would be drastically reduced from 6,400 to 900. The new authorities are also planning deportations for "bad behaviour". In terms of security policy, there is talk of authorizing searches in the absence of suspicious behaviour in certain neighbourhoods deemed sensitive, increasing the penalties and opening up the possibility of anonymous testimony in court. A national ban on begging will be tested. The agreement also provides for a downward revision of Sweden's [greenhouse gas emission](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") targets, a reduction in [development aid](/wiki/Development_aid "Development aid") and possible [privatization](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization").[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18)

With 19.1% of the vote for his party, Kristersson is the prime minister with the smallest amount of the vote since 1978. He began his term of office with a significantly lower popularity rating than that of the outgoing Prime Minister Magdalena Andersson.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18)

## Prime Minister of Sweden (2022–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Prime Minister of Sweden (2022–present)")]

See also: [Kristersson cabinet](/wiki/Kristersson_cabinet "Kristersson cabinet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Ebba_Busch_and_Ulf_Kristersson_in_2022.jpg/250px-Ebba_Busch_and_Ulf_Kristersson_in_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ebba_Busch_and_Ulf_Kristersson_in_2022.jpg)

Kristersson with [Deputy Prime Minister of Sweden](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Deputy Prime Minister of Sweden") [Ebba Busch](/wiki/Ebba_Busch "Ebba Busch") in 2022.

On 18 October 2022, Kristersson as per constitutional requirements, was officially identified by [King](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Sweden "Monarchy of Sweden") [Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf") as the new Prime Minister during a meeting of the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Sweden) "Council of State (Sweden)"), having announced his government program earlier that day in a speech to the Riksdag as well as having appointed the ministers of his cabinet.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Kristersson [reshuffled his cabinet for the first time](/wiki/2024_Swedish_cabinet_reshuffle "2024 Swedish cabinet reshuffle") on 10 September 2024 during his Declaration of Government speech at the [Riksdag Opening Ceremony](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Riksdag "Opening of the Riksdag").

### Domestic policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Domestic policy")]

#### Defence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Defence")]

Since May 2022, Sweden was in the process of [joining NATO](/wiki/Sweden-NATO_relations "Sweden-NATO relations"), after having received broad support in the Riksdag from both the then [government of Magdalena Andersson](/wiki/Andersson_Cabinet "Andersson Cabinet") and the four parties, then in opposition, now supporting Kristersson as prime minister. On 8 March 2023, the Kristersson government submitted the bill allowing Sweden's membership in NATO to the Riksdag, which then approved a revised version of the bill on 22 March 2023.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Sweden officially joined the alliance on 7 March 2024 when Kristersson signed and delivered the Instrument of Accession to US Secretary of State [Antony Blinken](/wiki/Antony_Blinken "Antony Blinken") during a press conference in Washington, D.C.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Later the same day he was invited to attend the [2024 State of the Union](/wiki/2024_State_of_the_Union "2024 State of the Union").[[57]](#cite_note-57) He also held a televised address to the nation to mark the occasion.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In the government's first budget, [Minister for Finance](/wiki/Minister_for_Finance_(Sweden) "Minister for Finance (Sweden)") [Elisabeth Svantesson](/wiki/Elisabeth_Svantesson "Elisabeth Svantesson") committed Sweden's defence expenditure to reach 2% of GDP by 2026. In line with this commitment, the government announced an increase in defence spending by 13.1 billion SEK, from 74.8 billion SEK to 87.9 billion SEK for the year 2023.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The budget included the establishment of the [Swedish Agency for Defence Analysis](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myndigheten_f%C3%B6r_totalf%C3%B6rsvarsanalys "sv:Myndigheten för totalförsvarsanalys") from 1 January 2023.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

On 2 December 2022, [Bergslagen Artillery Regiment](/wiki/Bergslagen_Artillery_Regiment "Bergslagen Artillery Regiment") (A9) was reopened after having been closed in 2000.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

In April and May 2023, the [Swedish Armed Forces](/wiki/Swedish_Armed_Forces "Swedish Armed Forces") executed the largest [military exercise](/wiki/Military_exercise "Military exercise") in Sweden in 34 years, [Aurora 23](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aurora_23 "sv:Aurora 23").[[62]](#cite_note-62) A total of 26,000 men and women participated in the exercise, which consisted of 14 participating countries and which involved all combat forces and defence branches. The exercise scenario was that a foreign power attacked Sweden on 24 April 2023.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

On 30 September 2024, Supreme Commander [Micael Bydén](/wiki/Micael_Byd%C3%A9n "Micael Bydén")'s commission is set to expire. This will end his tenure as Supreme Commander which has lasted since 2015. In June 2024 Kristersson announced his government had appointed Lieutenant General [Michael Claesson](/wiki/Michael_Claesson "Michael Claesson") as his successor to take office on 1 October 2024.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In 2025, Kristersson announced that his government had decided to go ahead with the largest rearmament of the Swedish Armed Forces since the Cold War. The plan is to over the next decade until 2035 loan 300 billion SEK to finance the venture.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Kristersson claimed after a meeting with opposition leaders that there was "broad unity" for the proposal.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Energy")]

During his first 100 days in office, Kristersson's government adopted policies that removed the bans on building new [nuclear reactors](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor"), increased funding to the [Swedish Radiation Safety Authority](/wiki/Swedish_Radiation_Safety_Authority "Swedish Radiation Safety Authority"), abolished taxes on [cogeneration](/wiki/Cogeneration "Cogeneration") electricity production, increased incentives for municipalities to approve the expansion [wind power plants](/wiki/Wind_power_plant "Wind power plant") and allocated 379 million [SEK](/wiki/Swedish_krona "Swedish krona") for investments in [energy efficiency](/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use") in single-family houses. The government also announced that Sweden's energy policy goal is to be changed from 100% "[renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy")" to 100% "[fossil free energy](/wiki/Fossil_fuel_phase-out "Fossil fuel phase-out")".[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In December 2022 and early January 2023, [state owned](/wiki/State_owned "State owned") energy company [Vattenfall](/wiki/Vattenfall "Vattenfall") as well as Finnish state owned energy company [Fortum](/wiki/Fortum "Fortum") both announced plans to build new nuclear reactors in Sweden.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

On 18 April 2023, [Vattenfall](/wiki/Vattenfall "Vattenfall") announced the largest expansion in Swedish [hydroelectric power](/wiki/Hydroelectric_power "Hydroelectric power") in over 30 years. The estimated production increase of the planned expansion is 720 MW, an increase by 9% of the companys current hydroelectric power production.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

On 27 April 2023, the government instructed the [Swedish Energy Agency](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energimyndigheten "sv:Energimyndigheten") to support the implementation of [stress tests in the energy sector](/wiki/Critical_infrastructure#Stress_testing "Critical infrastructure"). The purpose of the instruction is to increase the resilience in Sweden's energy sector and was partly in response to the [2022 Nord Stream pipeline sabotage](/wiki/2022_Nord_Stream_pipeline_sabotage "2022 Nord Stream pipeline sabotage").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

##### Response to the energy crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Response to the energy crisis")]

In response to the [global energy crisis](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932023_global_energy_crisis "2021–2023 global energy crisis"), on 27 October 2022, Kristersson and [Ebba Busch](/wiki/Ebba_Busch "Ebba Busch") announced a 55 billion (SEK) high-cost protection compensation in connection to the high increase of power bills for households; this first part of the protection was only paid out in the [energy price zones](/wiki/Electricity_price_area "Electricity price area") three and four (2 out of 4 zones) in the southern parts of Sweden.[[72]](#cite_note-72) On 9 January 2023, the government announced the next step in the compensation scheme which included all four energy price zones.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

To prevent the risk electricity-intensive production moving abroad in the event of bankruptcy due to high energy prices, the government announced on 22 December 2022 the introduction of high-cost protection compensation for electricity-intensive companies.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") approved the high-cost protection for companies on 16 February 2023.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

#### Migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Migration")]

During his first 100 days in office, Kristersson's government adopted policies in line with his stated paradigm shift in Swedish migration policies. This has included an increase by 25% of the number of internal controls on foreigners, a mission to authorities to intensify the work regarding the return of persons who do not meet the requirements to stay in Sweden, increased number of storage places, carrying out information efforts and analyze opportunities to increase voluntary [repatriation](/wiki/Repatriation "Repatriation") and lowered the level of [quota refugees](/wiki/Third_country_resettlement "Third country resettlement") to 900 per year (a decrease from 5,000 per year) and the [Swedish Migration Agency](/wiki/Swedish_Migration_Agency "Swedish Migration Agency") has been tasked with preparing for the changes.[[76]](#cite_note-Swedish_government_first_100_days_-_Migration-76)

On 5 April 2023, the government proposed a requirement that all asylum seekers, with the exception of [unaccompanied minors](/wiki/Unaccompanied_minor "Unaccompanied minor"), must live in state accommodation and participate in social introduction during the asylum process, that people who for some reason do not live in asylum accommodation should inform the Swedish Migration Agency of their [residential address](/wiki/Address "Address"). For asylum seekers who do not notify the Swedish Migration Agency, the authority must regard the asylum application as withdrawn, with the consequence that the asylum seeker needs to leave the country. The proposal also included that asylum seekers must be required to participate in [social introduction](/wiki/Culture_of_Sweden "Culture of Sweden") according to law. It is expected that the proposed changes will be law by 1 April 2024.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

In 2023 Sweden reported the lowest number of asylum seekers it had recorded during the 21st century. By August 2024 the number of asylum seekers had decreased by 27%. The Migration Agency predicts Sweden will in 2024 have the lowest number of immigrants since 1997, and will for the first time in over 50 years have more people migrating from Sweden than immigrating to Sweden.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

#### Crime prevention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Crime prevention")]

In the 2020s, violence of organized crime gangs escalated in Sweden; many people were shot. Kristersson announced during a speech to the nation in September 2023 that he would call in the country's armed forces for help. He would discuss with the heads of the *[Försvarsmakten](/wiki/Swedish_Armed_Forces "Swedish Armed Forces")* and the [police](/wiki/Swedish_Police_Authority "Swedish Police Authority") how soldiers could support police officers in their work against criminal gangs.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

To make cooperation between different government agencies easier when it comes to matters regarding national security Kristersson's cabinet established the [National Security Council](/wiki/National_Security_Council_(Sweden) "National Security Council (Sweden)").[[81]](#cite_note-81) Kristersson also appointed his childhood friend [Henrik Landerholm](/wiki/Henrik_Landerholm "Henrik Landerholm") as Sweden's first National Security Advisor.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

From 1 February 2024 the Police Authority can hand out "stay bans" to individuals promoting or taking part violence even if they haven't been convicted of any crime. A "stay ban" would ban the individual in question from entering and staying in a certain designated public area. The policy is supposed to increase safety standards in public spaces.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

On 25 April 2024 Kristersson's government implemented stop and search zones, referred to as Security Zones. In these temporary zones police are able to search people and vehicles without any suspicion of a crime on grounds such as wearing clothing brands associated with gangs and deviant behavior.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

On 1 January 2025, a law regarding anonymous witnesses was set into motion after years of debate, allowing witnesses to crimes the ability to inform authorities without sharing their identity. Kristersson pushed for this legal change within debates in the years leading up to this, and was one among many who proposed the law. One reason for this change was the fact that personal information in Sweden is public in the case of almost every citizen on sites such as [Hitta.se](/wiki/Hitta.se "Hitta.se") and [Eniro](/wiki/Eniro.se "Eniro.se"), where their addresses are also listed, leading to unhindered opportunities for convicted felons to retaliate against their witnesses. Kristersson, however, has not moved to alter this, unlike anonymous witnesses specifically. Despite the law being scheduled for 2025, Kristersson claimed in one speech prior to 1 January that the law was already in effect.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

#### Alcohol distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Alcohol distribution")]

In June 2024 Kristersson announced during a press conference that his government intended to introduce legislation allowing vineyards to in limited quantities sell their own liquor to guests in connection with lectures or guided tours of the property. This proposal is planned to take effect in 2025 and is not to affect [Systembolaget](/wiki/Systembolaget "Systembolaget")'s monopoly on selling liquor. The proposal would allow 600 vineyards and distilleries to distribute their liquor. Kristersson called the proposal a "freedom reform".[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

#### Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Economy")]

One of the early priorities of Kristersson's cabinet was the fight against inflation, which since 2021 had been above the [Riksbanks](/wiki/Sveriges_Riksbank "Sveriges Riksbank") goal rate of 2%, reaching as high as 12% in December 2022. By 2024 Finance Minister [Elisabeth Svantesson](/wiki/Elisabeth_Svantesson "Elisabeth Svantesson") reported that inflation had fallen to roughly 3%, with her and Kristersson claiming victory in the battle against inflation. The government said it would now shift its focus from battling inflation to creating and ensuring economic growth and fighting unemployment.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

In August 2024 Kristersson's government announced it would repeal the controversial Aviation Tax in their 2025 Spring Budget. This is supposed to enhance Sweden's competitiveness and decrease prices on commercial flights.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) The opposition, particularly the Green Party, criticised the government for this decision stating that it would increase carbon emissions and Sweden's environmental impacts. Companies within the aviation industry welcomed the decision.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

Kristersson with Finnish Prime Minister [Petteri Orpo](/wiki/Petteri_Orpo "Petteri Orpo") on 27 November 2023

On 8 November 2022, he travelled to Ankara to meet with [Turkish President](/wiki/Turkish_President "Turkish President") [Recep Tayyip Erdoğan](/wiki/Recep_Tayyip_Erdo%C4%9Fan "Recep Tayyip Erdoğan"). They spoke, among other things, about [Sweden's application to join NATO](/wiki/Sweden%E2%80%93NATO_relations#Membership "Sweden–NATO relations").[[100]](#cite_note-100) Kristersson promised to end support for the Kurdish [Kurdistan Workers' Party](/wiki/Kurdistan_Workers%27_Party "Kurdistan Workers' Party") (PKK) and [People's Defense Units](/wiki/People%27s_Defense_Units "People's Defense Units") (YPG) militia.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

On 1 January 2023, Sweden took over the [presidency of the Council of the European Union](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union") for a six-month term.[[102]](#cite_note-102) Prior to that, Sweden outlined four priorities for their presidency:[[103]](#cite_note-103)

* Security – Unity
* Resilience – Competitiveness
* Prosperity – Green transition and energy transition
* Democratic values and the rule of law – Our foundation

On 15 February 2023, Kristersson visited [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") and met with President [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy") to discuss about the ongoing [Russian invasion](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"). Kristersson stressed that "Ukraine belongs to Europe and belongs in the European Union". During a joint press meeting, Kristersson underlined that [Sweden intends to continue with political, economic and military support to Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_military_aid_to_Ukraine_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War#S "List of military aid to Ukraine during the Russo-Ukrainian War").[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)

In April 2023, after approval from the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag"), Kristersson's cabinet authorised a [military evacuation operation](/wiki/Military_intervention "Military intervention") that evacuated Swedish diplomatic staff from the [Swedish Embassy in Khartoum](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sveriges_ambassad_i_Khartoum "sv:Sveriges ambassad i Khartoum") and their families from Sudan, as well as a large number of other Swedish citizens currently staying in [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan"). In addition, a number of Swedish citizens left Sudan in other ways and over 100 other foreign citizens were evacuated by Swedish flights. The reason for the evacuation operation was the outbreak of the [2023 Sudan conflict](/wiki/2023_Sudan_conflict "2023 Sudan conflict").[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In late 2023, the Kristersson cabinet failed to evacuate Swedish dual citizens from the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") following the outbreak of the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war").[[109]](#cite_note-109) In November 2023, Kristersson said that Israel has the right to self-defence.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

In July 2024, Kristersson attended the [2024 Washington summit](/wiki/2024_Washington_summit "2024 Washington summit"), the first NATO summit attended by Sweden as an official member of the alliance.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

In November 2024, Kristersson congratulated [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") for his victory in the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), but also noted the risks associated with another Trump term. He declared changes in US aid to Ukraine in the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") as the largest risk, and suggested increasing European support to Ukraine. Another worry Kristersson expressed pertained to Trump's actions and promises regarding the [Paris Climate Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement").[[112]](#cite_note-112)

In December 2024, Kristersson cancelled a scheduled visit to [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") as a result of President [Yoon Suk-Yeol](/wiki/Yoon_Suk-Yeol "Yoon Suk-Yeol")'s [decision to impose martial law](/wiki/2024_South_Korean_martial_law "2024 South Korean martial law") and the subsequent developments.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

On 2 March 2025, Kristersson attended a [meeting of European leaders](/wiki/2025_London_Summit_on_Ukraine "2025 London Summit on Ukraine") led by British Prime Minister [Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") in London to discuss the security situation in Ukraine. Kristersson described the meeting as "much more important after yesterday's events in Washington, D.C.", referring to the [failed meeting between U.S President Trump and Ukrainian President Zelenskyy](/wiki/2025_Trump-Zelenskyy_meeting "2025 Trump-Zelenskyy meeting").[[114]](#cite_note-114)

In January 2026, following the [capture](/wiki/2026_United_States_intervention_in_Venezuela "2026 United States intervention in Venezuela") of [Venezuelan](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") President [Nicolás Maduro](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro "Nicolás Maduro") by U.S. forces, Kristersson expressed a nuanced stance that balanced support for democratic transition with concerns over international legal standards. In January 2026, Kristersson reaffirmed Sweden's support for Denmark's and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland")'s sovereignty amidst renewed [U.S. interest in acquiring Greenland](/wiki/Proposed_United_States_acquisition_of_Greenland "Proposed United States acquisition of Greenland").[[115]](#cite_note-115)

On 28 February 2026, following joint [U.S.–Israeli strikes on Iran](/wiki/2026_Iran_War "2026 Iran War"), Kristersson expressed support for Sweden's allies while criticizing the Iranian regime's oppression of its citizens. He characterized Iran’s [nuclear program](/wiki/Nuclear_program_of_Iran "Nuclear program of Iran") and its [support for militant groups](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Israel_proxy_conflict "Iran–Israel proxy conflict") as long-term destabilizing factors in the Middle East.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

## Political positions and image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Political positions and image")]

A 2018 political profile in *[The Local](/wiki/The_Local "The Local")* described Kristersson as exuding "nice guy vibes: smart, humble and reasonable, easygoing and open to discussion" while positioning him to the right of his predecessors on issues such as crime and immigration. He was also described in the same profile as representing the [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") wing of the [Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party") (M).[[117]](#cite_note-117)

[![Kristersson with German Chancellor Angela Merkel in 2017.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/A23A1112_%2823940224608%29.jpg/250px-A23A1112_%2823940224608%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A23A1112_(23940224608).jpg)

Kristersson with then-German Chancellor [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel") in 2017

Kristersson himself has described [social mobility](/wiki/Social_mobility "Social mobility") as one of his core concerns in politics.[[118]](#cite_note-118) In his first leadership speech, Kristersson stated that Sweden should become "a country for hopefuls" and that the Moderate Party should be "a party for hopefuls".[[119]](#cite_note-119) On the matter of asylum, Kristersson states that he supports the integration of refugees into Swedish society but argues for compulsory [cultural assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation")[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] and learning of the [Swedish language](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), and that refugees be put to work and pay tax.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Kristersson initially ruled out forming an alliance with the [Sweden Democrats](/wiki/Sweden_Democrats "Sweden Democrats") (SD) upon assuming party leadership; following the [2018 Swedish general election](/wiki/2018_Swedish_general_election "2018 Swedish general election"), he ended the policy of non-cooperation and met with SD's leadership for official talks.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122) Before the [2022 Swedish general election](/wiki/2022_Swedish_general_election "2022 Swedish general election"), Kristersson suggested that he would form a loose right-wing bloc consisting of M, the [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democrats_(Sweden) "Christian Democrats (Sweden)") (KD), [Liberals](/wiki/Liberals_(Sweden) "Liberals (Sweden)") (L) and SD but expressed uncertainty at SD's demand that they be allocated cabinet positions should the right-wing bloc win a majority.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Following the election, Kristersson signalled his ambition to form a new conservative government with support from SD.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In 2023, following a series of [Quran burnings in Sweden](/wiki/2023_Quran_burnings_in_Sweden "2023 Quran burnings in Sweden"), a trend started by Danish-Swedish politician [Rasmus Paludan](/wiki/Rasmus_Paludan "Rasmus Paludan") which garnered international attention, Kristersson publicly denounced these actions.[[125]](#cite_note-125) However, he affirmed his belief in freedom of expression, asserting that despite his condemnation, he acknowledges the legality of the burning of any book including holy ones.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ulf_Kristersson&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Kristersson lives in [Strängnäs](/wiki/Str%C3%A4ngn%C3%A4s "Strängnäs").[[127]](#cite_note-127) He has been married since 1991 to [Birgitta Ed](/wiki/Birgitta_Ed "Birgitta Ed") (b. 1968), formerly a public relations consultant and as of 2023 a priest in the clergy of the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"). They have [adopted three daughters from China](/wiki/International_adoption "International adoption").[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) Kristersson does not consider himself a religious believer.[[130]](#cite_note-130)
